nativeRange.habitat: Clear waters on well-developed coral reefs, reef channels connecting lagoons to the open ocean, and seaward reef slopes; reported at depths of roughly 3–40 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on sponges and tunicates supplemented by algae. In captivity, tends to accept a varied diet including high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge material, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, marine algae, and spirulina. Feeding small portions two to three times daily is generally advised to reduce the likelihood of coral nipping.

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker; 2048x1991. Sharp side profile of a live adult showing dense golden spots, blue lips, and black-edged fins with pale margins against a reef backdrop; best diagnostic detail and most natural colour.
NOAA Photo Library; 4000x3000. Full-body in-situ reef shot including tail, showing golden spotting, blue lips and blue-white fin margins; slight underwater teal cast.
Jim E. Maragos, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service; 1750x1368. Sharp full side profile with vivid golden spots, blue lips and black-edged fins; green underwater colour cast keeps it as a secondary pick.

summary: A striking large angelfish from the Central Pacific, recognized by its golden-spotted brown body, blue lips, and black-edged fins. Generally considered intermediate in care and best kept singly in a well-established reef with ample live rock.
careNotes:
The Goldflake Angelfish is generally considered intermediate in difficulty and reaches around 25 cm (approximately 10 inches) as an adult. It tends to be semi-aggressive and territorial once established, and is best housed one per tank; aggression toward other angelfish and toward conspecifics can be significant. A mature, well-established reef aquarium with plenty of live rock for grazing and several caves or crevices for shelter is generally advised. Moderate to strong water flow is commonly recommended to simulate natural reef conditions.

This species is generally considered reef-safe with caution. Most individuals have been reported to leave corals undisturbed, but isolated cases of nipping at clam mantles (particularly Tridacna), LPS corals (large-polyp stony corals), SPS corals (small-polyp stony corals), and some soft corals have been documented. Captive-bred specimens tend to show fewer nipping behaviours than wild-caught individuals.

Recommended minimum tank sizes vary considerably across hobby sources. Retailers commonly cite around 125–130 US gallons (approximately 473–492 L), while specialist databases suggest as much as 800 L to accommodate the fish's active swimming behaviour and adult size. A minimum of 500 L (approximately 130 US gallons) is a reasonable baseline, though erring larger is advisable.

The Goldflake Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and may later change sex to male — a trait common to the angelfish family (Pomacanthidae). It is diurnal, meaning active during the day. The species was historically rare in the aquarium trade but has become increasingly available in recent years, including captive-bred specimens from a growing number of aquaculture programs.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely reported and is considered very difficult; commercial captive-bred availability is limited. No established captive breeding protocol is documented in mainstream hobbyist or scientific literature.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperament, reef-safe status, native range. Gave minimum tank as 75 gal vs LiveAquaria 130 gal — wide disagreement; final value biased larger per playbook rules.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (74–82°F), pH (8.1–8.5), salinity (1.020–1.026), tank size (125 gal), care level (intermediate/moderate). Lifespan listed as 5 years.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Specialist database recommends 800 L minimum — substantially larger than hobby sources. Confirmed adult size 25 cm and semi-aggressive temperament. Used to anchor the larger end of tank-size range.

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Caroline, Gilbert, Phoenix, Line Islands), adult size 25 cm, protogynous hermaphroditism, diurnal behaviour, and sponge/tunicate diet.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely reported; no established protocol exists. Commercial captive-bred availability is limited but growing.
